children.name: Tiliqua
children.description: Blue-tongued skinks of Australia.

children.name: Tiliqua scincoides
children.description: Flashes vivid cobalt-blue tongue as a threat display contrasting with the pink mouth; gives birth to live young; widely kept as a pet; ground-dwelling in open woodland.

children.name: Tiliqua gigas
children.description: Similar to Eastern; New Guinea and Maluku Islands.

children.name: Tiliqua rugosa
children.description: Stout, slow-moving skink with rough, pine-cone-like scales and a stumpy tail that mimics the head; forms long-term monogamous pair bonds unique among lizards. Also called bobtail or pinecone lizard.

children.name: Tiliqua adelaidensis
children.description: Presumed extinct for decades until rediscovered in 1992 living exclusively inside spider burrows in native grasslands north of Adelaide. Critically dependent on trapdoor spider burrows for thermoregulation and protection.

children.name: Tiliqua multifasciata
children.description: Inhabits arid interior Australia; banded pattern distinguishes it from the eastern species. Viviparous like all blue-tongues, producing small litters in late summer.

children.name: Tiliqua nigrolutea
children.description: Found in cool montane and coastal heathland of south-eastern Australia and Tasmania; distinctive cream and dark blotched pattern. Tolerates lower temperatures than most skinks and is active on mild overcast days.

children.name: Plestiodon
children.description: North American skinks.

children.name: Plestiodon fasciatus
children.description: Juveniles have iridescent electric-blue tails to draw predator attention; common under logs in eastern forests; tail autotomy standard defence.

children.name: Plestiodon laticeps
children.description: Males develop bright orange-red heads in breeding season; arboreal.

children.name: Plestiodon obsoletus
children.description: Largest North American skink, reaching 35 cm; inhabits rocky hillsides and grasslands of the Great Plains and Mexican plateau. Female guards egg clutch until hatching, unusual maternal behaviour for a lizard.

children.name: Plestiodon inexpectatus
children.description: Nearly identical to P. fasciatus but distinguishable by scale counts; confined to southeastern coastal plain. Often found in pine flatwoods and under rotting logs near moisture.

children.name: Plestiodon skiltonianus
children.description: Slender species of western North America from British Columbia to Baja California; frequents rocky chaparral and mixed forest edges. Named after Darius Nash Skilton, an American naturalist who collected specimens in Oregon.

children.name: Plestiodon anthracinus
children.description: Small secretive skink of moist wooded hillsides and streambanks in the eastern US, named for its charcoal-grey colour. Two disjunct subspecies separated by hundreds of kilometres; the species may represent multiple cryptic species.

children.name: Plestiodon callicephalus
children.description: A small, secretive skink of the Madrean sky islands of Arizona, New Mexico, and northern Mexico, inhabiting oak woodland and pine-oak forest. Named for its beautifully marked head. Prefers rocky streamsides with overhead canopy.

children.name: Plestiodon egregius
children.description: A small, semi-fossorial skink endemic to Florida and nearby coastal islands, with a distinctive reddish tail. Burrows in loose sandy soils of coastal dunes, scrub, and pine flatwoods. Several subspecies are isolated on barrier islands and some are considered threatened.

children.name: Plestiodon multivirgatus
children.description: A slender skink of shortgrass prairies and pinon-juniper woodlands in the western Great Plains and Rocky Mountain foothills. Named for its many narrow pale stripes on a dark background. Diurnal and active, feeding on grasshoppers and beetles.

children.name: Plestiodon septentrionalis
children.description: A medium-sized skink of the central Great Plains, from southern Canada to Texas. Named for its northern distribution relative to other Plestiodon. Prefers open grassland with sandy soils where it burrows and hunts invertebrates.

children.name: Scincus
children.description: Desert sand-swimming skinks.

children.name: Scincus scincus
children.description: Swims through loose desert sand using sinusoidal body movements; polished overlapping scales minimise friction; can dive below the surface in under a second to escape heat or predators.

children.name: Scincus mitranus
children.description: Restricted to the sand deserts of the Arabian Peninsula and adjacent Iran; slightly more robust than S. scincus with a broader snout adapted for sand-diving. Active mainly at dawn and dusk to avoid extreme midday heat.

children.name: Scincus albifasciatus
children.description: A Saharan sandfish with contrasting white bands across the body; endemic to the sand seas of Mauritania, Algeria, and Libya. Swims through loose sand with extraordinary speed using undulatory body movements.

children.name: Scincus hemprichii
children.description: A sand-swimming skink of the Arabian Peninsula and southern Iran and Iraq, named after the German naturalist Friedrich Wilhelm Hemprich. Distinguished from S. scincus by a slightly more elongated snout and different scale row counts.

children.name: Eutropis
children.description: Sun skinks of Asia.

children.name: Eutropis multifasciata
children.description: Extremely abundant in SE Asian gardens and farmland; active basker on rocks and logs.

children.name: Eutropis macularia
children.description: Widely distributed from Sri Lanka and India across Southeast Asia; metallic bronze flanks with scattered dark spots give it its common name. A diurnal insectivore of open grassland, gardens, and forest clearings.

children.name: Eutropis longicaudatus
children.description: One of the larger Asian sun skinks, with a tail roughly twice the body length; inhabits secondary forest and scrub in Indochina and Thailand. Active forager of invertebrates in leaf litter.

children.name: Eutropis cumingi
children.description: A Philippine endemic sun skink named after the English naturalist Hugh Cuming. Found across most Philippine islands in open habitats, forest edges, and gardens. Similar to E. multifasciata but with distinct scale characteristics.

children.name: Eutropis rugifera
children.description: A sun skink of the Malay Peninsula, Sumatra, and Borneo, distinguished by keeled, somewhat rugose dorsal scales. Inhabits lowland primary and secondary forests, often seen basking on fallen logs.

children.name: Eutropis dissimilis
children.description: A medium-sized sun skink of South Asia from Pakistan through India to Myanmar. Favours open grassy terrain and agricultural margins where it hunts insects in dense ground cover. Males develop bright orange throats in breeding season.

children.name: Mabuya
children.description: New World skinks.

children.name: Mabuya mabouya
children.description: One of the few reptiles with true placental reproduction; embryos receive all nutrition via placenta as in mammals.

children.name: Mabuya heathi
children.description: Endemic to the caatinga and semi-arid scrub of northeastern Brazil; like other Mabuya it is viviparous with a highly developed chorioallantoic placenta. Named after Harold Heath, American biologist.

children.name: Trachylepis
children.description: African sun skinks.

children.name: Trachylepis striata
children.description: Common in sub-Saharan Africa; rock-dwelling; speed main defence.

children.name: Trachylepis quinquetaeniata
children.description: Bright five-striped juvenile coloration fades with age; widespread from Egypt through East Africa and into Arabia. Commonly seen basking on stone walls and termite mounds.

children.name: Trachylepis varia
children.description: Extremely variable in pattern across its wide sub-Saharan range; often found on rocky outcrops and kopjes in grassland. One of the most ecologically flexible skinks on the continent.

children.name: Trachylepis spilogaster
children.description: Inhabits dry savanna and semi-desert from Sudan and Ethiopia through the Horn of Africa; slender body with spotted underside. Active forager that retreats quickly into rock crevices when threatened.

children.name: Trachylepis maculilabris
children.description: A common West and Central African skink, from Senegal to Angola, named for the white spots on the lips. Highly adaptable to disturbed habitats including urban areas, where it is frequently seen on walls and garden rocks. Diurnal insectivore.

children.name: Trachylepis sulcata
children.description: A rock-dwelling skink of southern and southwestern Africa, from Namibia to the Cape. Deeply grooved dorsal scales give it a textured, almost sculpted appearance. Shelters in deep rock crevices and emerges to bask on exposed rock faces.

children.name: Trachylepis capensis
children.description: A large, robust skink of South Africa and Namibia, reaching 25 cm. Inhabits a wide range of habitats from fynbos to Karoo scrub. One of the most frequently seen lizards in the Western Cape.

children.name: Ctenotus
children.description: Striped skinks, the most species-rich lizard genus in Australia.

children.name: Ctenotus taeniolatus
children.description: One of the most familiar Australian skinks; boldly striped with a reddish-copper tail; abundant in coastal heath and woodland. A swift diurnal forager of insects and small invertebrates.

children.name: Ctenotus robustus
children.description: Largest and most powerfully built of the Ctenotus skinks; widespread across eastern and central Australia in a variety of dry habitats. Feeds on larger arthropods and occasionally small lizards.

children.name: Ctenotus pantherinus
children.description: Striking species with a complex pattern of spots, ocelli, and stripes across the back; found in arid interior Australia in spinifex grassland. One of the more ornate members of its genus.

children.name: Ctenotus regius
children.description: A mid-sized Ctenotus of the arid and semi-arid zones with clean pale stripes on a dark dorsum; typically found in sandy soils with spinifex. Fast and wary, retreating into grass tussocks when disturbed.

children.name: Ctenotus fallens
children.description: A fast, diurnal skink of southwestern Australia, inhabiting coastal heath and woodland. One of the most common reptiles in the Perth region. Two pale stripes on a brown body.

children.name: Ctenotus grandis
children.description: One of the largest Ctenotus species, reaching 12 cm snout-vent length. Found in arid interior Australia across Western Australia, South Australia, and the Northern Territory. Boldly striped with a pale vertebral stripe.

children.name: Ctenotus leonhardii
children.description: A common Ctenotus of the central Australian arid zone, named after the Finnish explorer and naturalist Leonard (Leonhardi) of the Hamburg South Australian expedition. Five narrow stripes on a dark background.

children.name: Ctenotus helenae
children.description: A medium-sized Ctenotus from the Pilbara and adjacent arid regions of Western Australia, named after Helen, daughter of the describing herpetologist. Uniquely patterned with a pale vertebral stripe and bold lateral flecks.

children.name: Lerista
children.description: Sand-swimmers with reduced limbs, illustrating limb loss in action.

children.name: Lerista bougainvillii
children.description: Named after French navigator Louis Antoine de Bougainville; one of the larger Lerista with reduced but functional limbs. Occupies sandy soils in coastal and sub-coastal southern Australia.

children.name: Lerista punctatovittata
children.description: Slender burrowing skink of arid South Australia and Western Australia with vestigial hindlimbs; dorsum bears rows of small dark dots. Spends most of its time beneath leaf litter or in loose sandy soil.

children.name: Lerista lineata
children.description: A slender, limb-reduced burrowing skink of the Swan Coastal Plain around Perth, Western Australia. Deeply fossorial, spending virtually its entire life burrowing through loose sandy soils. Listed as Vulnerable due to urban development destroying its restricted habitat.

children.name: Lerista labialis
children.description: A small burrowing skink of arid and semi-arid southern Australia, from Western Australia through South Australia to Victoria. Reduced limbs with only three toes; shows the characteristic Lerista body plan adapted for sand-swimming.

children.name: Lerista desertorum
children.description: A highly specialised sand-swimmer of the central Australian deserts, with the most extreme limb reduction in the genus — the forelimbs are completely absent. Moves through loose sand with serpentine locomotion, rarely if ever emerging to the surface.

children.name: Cryptoblepharus
children.description: Snake-eyed skinks with transparent fixed eyelids, found on rocky coasts and islands.

children.name: Cryptoblepharus boutonii
children.description: A remarkably widespread species found on rocky shores and coral islands from East Africa to the Pacific; fused, transparent lower eyelid gives the eye a snake-like appearance. Highly salt-tolerant and often seen foraging in the intertidal zone.

children.name: Cryptoblepharus virgatus
children.description: Common on rock faces and tree trunks in eastern Australia; adhesive toe pads allow it to hunt on vertical surfaces. One of Australia's most frequently observed skinks in coastal areas.

children.name: Cryptoblepharus leschenaultii
children.description: Named after Jean-Baptiste Leschenault de la Tour, French botanist on Baudin's expedition; found from India through Southeast Asia to northern Australia. Lives on the trunks of large trees and rocky outcrops.

children.name: Cryptoblepharus australis
children.description: A pale snake-eyed skink of interior Australia, found on rock faces and tree trunks in the arid zone. Fused transparent eyelids give its eye a distinctive snake-like appearance. Highly alert and fast-moving.

children.name: Cryptoblepharus plagiocephalus
children.description: A common snake-eyed skink of northern Australia, named for its somewhat flattened head profile. Forages on tree trunks and rock outcrops in tropical savanna and woodland. One of the most abundant reptiles of the Top End.

children.name: Emoia
children.description: Pacific skinks highly diverse across Melanesia and Oceania.

children.name: Emoia cyanura
children.description: Brilliant sky-blue tail contrasts with the bronze-brown body; abundant in coconut plantations and disturbed coastal habitats across the Pacific. A highly successful coloniser of oceanic islands.

children.name: Emoia atrocostata
children.description: Specialised for life on rocky coasts and intertidal boulders from the Philippines to the Solomon Islands; dark coloration absorbs heat rapidly in shaded coastal zones. Can withstand brief wave immersion.

children.name: Emoia caeruleocauda
children.description: One of the most widespread Emoia across Pacific island groups from New Guinea to Samoa and Fiji; vivid blue tail particularly bright in juveniles. Adapts readily to gardens and village environments.

children.name: Emoia jakati
children.description: A medium-sized Emoia of the New Guinea region and Solomon Islands, with a coppery-brown body and scattered dark flecks. Active in leaf litter in both primary and secondary lowland forests. Named after the local term in the region.

children.name: Emoia tropidolepis
children.description: An Emoia from the highlands of New Guinea with strongly keeled dorsal scales, unique within the genus. Inhabits montane forest above 1,000 m, where it basks on mossy logs and tree ferns.

children.name: Emoia physicae
children.description: A small Emoia of the Bismarck Archipelago and Solomon Islands. Inhabits lowland rainforest and gardens, often seen on low vegetation. Named for the descriptive Latin term relating to its body form.

children.name: Carlia
children.description: Rainbow skinks of Australia and New Guinea with iridescent colouration.

children.name: Carlia tetradactyla
children.description: Slender, fast-moving skink of coastal heathland in Queensland and New South Wales; males develop vivid orange flanks in breeding season. Named for having four toes on each foot.

children.name: Carlia fusca
children.description: Widespread in northern Australia and New Guinea in wet tropical and monsoon forest edges; dark dorsal flecks over a brown background. An agile insectivore most active in morning sun.

children.name: Carlia amax
children.description: A small rainbow skink of the Kimberley region and Top End of northern Australia. Males develop brilliant orange and blue flank colours in breeding season. Named for the two prominent preanal scales.

children.name: Carlia pectoralis
children.description: A rainbow skink of open forests in Queensland and New South Wales, with a distinct white stripe along the side and strong sexual dichromatism. Males have vivid orange-red sides. Active on leaf litter in sunlit patches.

children.name: Carlia gracilis
children.description: A slender, long-tailed Carlia of the Kimberley region in northern Western Australia. The most gracile member of the genus, with a particularly long tail reaching twice the body length.

children.name: Sphenomorphus
children.description: Forest skinks of Asia and Australasia, among the most species-rich skink genera.

children.name: Sphenomorphus indicus
children.description: Common in leaf litter of Indian and Southeast Asian forests; secretive burrower that hunts small invertebrates under decaying logs. Smooth brown scales provide excellent camouflage among forest debris.

children.name: Sphenomorphus scutatus
children.description: Robust forest skink of montane forests in Borneo and the Philippines; enlarged shield-like dorsal scales give it its name. A slow-moving, largely fossorial species found under bark and logs.

children.name: Sphenomorphus incognitus
children.description: A slender brown skink of Southeast Asian forests, from Myanmar through Thailand to Malaysia. Highly cryptic in leaf litter, with an unmarked bronze-brown dorsum. Formerly confused with S. indicus.

children.name: Sphenomorphus jobiensis
children.description: A medium-sized Sphenomorphus from New Guinea and surrounding islands, including Jobi (Yapen) Island. Inhabits lowland and hill forests, active in leaf litter. Part of a complex of closely related species.

children.name: Sphenomorphus simus
children.description: A robust Sphenomorphus of the Philippines and Borneo with a distinctively short, blunt snout. Found in montane and lowland rainforests. Forages on the forest floor among fallen leaves and decaying wood.

children.name: Lygosoma
children.description: Cylindrical burrowing skinks of Africa and Asia; gave name to the old subfamily Lygosominae.

children.name: Lygosoma punctata
children.description: Worm-like in proportions; smooth, highly polished scales enable rapid movement through damp soil and leaf litter across South and Southeast Asia. Largely fossorial, emerging at dusk to hunt small invertebrates.

children.name: Oligosoma
children.description: New Zealand skinks, the dominant lizard genus of Aotearoa.

children.name: Oligosoma grande
children.description: New Zealand's largest skink, up to 27 cm; endemic to rocky schist outcrops of Otago; classified Endangered due to predation by introduced stoats, rats, and cats. Forms stable social groups with pair bonds lasting several years.

children.name: Oligosoma otagense
children.description: Co-occurs with the Grand Skink on schist rock outcrops in Central Otago, New Zealand; bold black-and-yellow pattern on a greenish background. Critically threatened by mammalian predators introduced to New Zealand.

children.name: Oligosoma infrapunctatum
children.description: Widespread across both main islands of New Zealand in grassland, scrub, and coastal habitats; underside spotted with pale flecks. One of the more common mainland skinks of Aotearoa.

children.name: Oligosoma nigriplantare
children.description: Widespread on coastal rocks and sandy shores throughout New Zealand; sole of foot is black, giving the species name. Often found sheltering under driftwood above the tideline.

children.name: Niveoscincus
children.description: Snow skinks of Tasmania and the Australian Alps, cold-climate specialists.

children.name: Niveoscincus ocellatus
children.description: Most widespread and common of the Tasmanian snow skinks; dorsal pattern of pale-edged dark spots (ocelli) makes it easy to identify. Found from sea level to alpine heath, tolerating snow for weeks.

children.name: Niveoscincus metallicus
children.description: Iridescent coppery-bronze scales reflect sunlight; found in cool temperate forests and alpine areas of Tasmania and the Bass Strait islands. Basks extensively on rocks to maintain body temperature in cool climates.

children.name: Corucia
children.description: Monotypic genus; the largest living skink in the world.

children.name: Corucia zebrata
children.description: The world's largest skink at up to 76 cm; endemic to the Solomon Islands; prehensile tail used to grip branches like a fifth limb. Strictly herbivorous and arboreal, living in family groups with extended parental care rare in reptiles.

children.name: Egernia
children.description: Australian social skinks renowned for complex family structures.

children.name: Egernia cunninghami
children.description: Large, spiny-scaled skink named after Allan Cunningham, British botanist who collected in Australia. Lives in stable family groups in rocky crevices on granite outcrops; viviparous with strong social bonds between adults and offspring.

children.name: Egernia stokesii
children.description: Named after John Lort Stokes, British naval officer who charted Australian coasts; inhabits arid mulga and gidgee scrubland. Shelters in communal rock crevices with spiny tail used to block the entrance to the burrow.

children.name: Egernia whitii
children.description: Named after John White, surgeon-general on the First Fleet who described Australian fauna; widespread in rocky habitats of south-eastern Australia. Lives in stable multi-generational family units, one of the best-studied examples of sociality in lizards.

children.name: Egernia depressa
children.description: A small, flattened egernia of arid interior Australia, from the Pilbara to the western deserts. Dorsoventrally compressed body allows it to shelter in narrow rock crevices. Spiny tail used to plug the crevice entrance. Viviparous.

children.name: Egernia hosmeri
children.description: A medium-sized egernia of tropical northern Australia, from the Kimberley across the Top End. Named after the Australian zoologist Robert Hosmer. Lives in family groups in rock piles and crevices. Viviparous with strong social structure.

children.name: Egernia napoleonis
children.description: A large, spiny egernia of southwestern Australia, named after Napoleon Bonaparte by French naturalists of Baudin's expedition. Inhabits granite outcrops and rocky slopes in jarrah and karri forests. Lives in multi-generational social groups.

children.name: Tribolonotus
children.description: Crocodile skinks of New Guinea and the Solomon Islands, armoured with keeled scales.

children.name: Tribolonotus gracilis
children.description: Distinctive bright orange-red eye ring and four rows of keeled, spine-like scales along the back give it a prehistoric appearance; endemic to New Guinea. Emits a loud squeak when handled and will play dead, unusual defensive behaviours for a lizard.

children.name: Tribolonotus novaeguineae
children.description: Inhabits humid tropical forest floor of New Guinea; armoured dorsal scales arranged in neat rows recalling crocodilian osteoderms. Largely nocturnal and secretive under fallen logs near streams.

children.name: Prasinohaema
children.description: Green-blooded skinks of New Guinea with biliverdin-saturated blood.

children.name: Prasinohaema virens
children.description: The most studied of the green-blooded skinks; blood, muscles, bones, and tongue are all vivid lime-green due to extraordinary concentrations of biliverdin that would be toxic in most vertebrates. Found in montane forests of New Guinea; the adaptive benefit of the green blood is still debated.

children.name: Prasinohaema prehensicauda
children.description: Prehensile tail assists climbing in the understorey of New Guinean montane rainforest; shares the green blood pigmentation of all Prasinohaema. Arboreal habits reduce competition with ground-dwelling relatives.

children.name: Eumeces
children.description: Old World skinks, formerly a broad catch-all genus now restricted to Asia and North Africa.

children.name: Eumeces schneideri
children.description: One of the largest Old World skinks, reaching 40 cm; vivid orange lateral spots brighten during breeding season; found in North Africa and the Middle East to Central Asia. Partially fossorial and omnivorous, eating plant matter as well as insects.

children.name: Eumeces taeniolatus
children.description: Slender Asian species with pale dorsal stripes on a dark brown background; distributed from Iran through Pakistan and Afghanistan into India. Inhabits rocky semi-desert and scrub where it shelters under flat stones.

children.name: Eumeces algeriensis
children.description: A large North African skink of coastal Algeria, Tunisia, and Morocco, closely related to the Berber skink. Vivid orange-red spots on the flanks are particularly intense in breeding adults. Inhabits rocky Mediterranean maquis and hillsides.

children.name: Eumeces blythianus
children.description: A moderate-sized skink of the Indus Valley and western India, named after Edward Blyth, English zoologist and curator of the Asiatic Society of Bengal. Found in arid and semi-arid habitats with loose, sandy soils where it burrows.

children.name: Lipinia
children.description: Small forest-floor skinks of Southeast Asia and Pacific islands.

children.name: Lipinia noctua
children.description: Tiny yellow-striped skink found across a vast arc of Pacific islands from the Marianas to Fiji; among the most successfully distributed small lizards in Oceania. Often associated with coconut husks and forest edge habitats.

children.name: Lankascincus
children.description: Sri Lanka endemic skinks of forest and plantation habitats.

children.name: Lankascincus fallax
children.description: The most widespread and frequently encountered skink on the island of Sri Lanka; found from sea level to mid-elevation forests and coconut plantations. Slender and quick, foraging actively by day among leaf litter.

children.name: Lankascincus dorsalis
children.description: Endemic to Sri Lanka's wet zone forests; distinguished from its congeners by a prominent pale dorsal stripe running the length of the body. Threatened by deforestation of lowland rainforest.

children.name: Chalcides
children.description: A genus of skinks with highly reduced or absent limbs that resemble small snakes or slow worms. They are found across the Mediterranean region, North Africa, the Middle East, and Macaronesia. Many species are live-bearing (viviparous), an adaptation to cool mountain climates. Limb reduction within Chalcides is a textbook example of convergent evolution with other legless lizard groups.

children.name: Chalcides chalcides
children.description: A cylindrical, snake-like skink of Italy, Corsica, Sardinia, and Sicily, with only three tiny toes on each foot. Moves through grass using lateral undulation, resembling a slow worm. Viviparous, giving birth to live young in late summer.

children.name: Chalcides ocellatus
children.description: A chunky, short-limbed skink found from Greece and Italy through North Africa to Arabia and Iran. Named for the pale ocellated (eye-like) spots along the flanks. One of the most widely distributed Mediterranean skinks, inhabiting dry scrub, olive groves, and sandy coastal areas.

children.name: Chalcides viridanus
children.description: An endemic skink of Tenerife in the Canary Islands, with a greenish-bronze dorsum that gives it its name. Found from sea level to high-altitude pine forests in the Teide region. Viviparous like many Chalcides.

children.name: Chalcides striatus
children.description: A slender, snake-like skink of the Iberian Peninsula and southern France, with three toes on each tiny limb. Inhabits damp meadows, marsh edges, and shady hedgerows. Gives birth to live young; the young are born enclosed in a transparent membrane that they rupture immediately.

children.name: Chalcides bedriagai
children.description: A small, fossorial skink of the Iberian Peninsula, named after Jacques Bedriaga, Russian herpetologist who studied Mediterranean reptiles. Highly reduced limbs with only two or three toes; burrows in loose, sandy soils. Listed as Near Threatened due to habitat loss from intensive agriculture.

children.name: Mochlus
children.description: A genus of African skinks found primarily in the savanna and forest regions south of the Sahara. They have a characteristic flexible, snake-like body with reduced limbs adapted for moving through leaf litter and loose soil. Formerly included within Lygosoma but now recognised as a distinct genus.

children.name: Mochlus sundevallii
children.description: A small, cylindrical skink of savanna and dry forests across sub-Saharan Africa, from Senegal to South Africa. Named after Carl Jakob Sundevall, Swedish zoologist. Reduced limbs and a flexible body allow it to 'swim' through leaf litter and loose soil.

children.name: Mochlus fernandi
children.description: A brightly coloured supple skink of West and Central African rainforests, with a vivid pattern of alternating dark and light longitudinal stripes. Named after Fernando Po (Bioko Island), where it was first collected.

children.name: Mochlus laevis
children.description: A medium-sized supple skink of Tanzania, Malawi, and Zambia, with particularly smooth, glossy scales. Inhabits miombo woodland and savanna where it hunts insects and small invertebrates in leaf litter.

children.name: Panaspis
children.description: Small, secretive skinks of sub-Saharan Africa with fused, transparent eyelids that give them a snake-like appearance. They are ground-dwellers of forest floors and savanna leaf litter, feeding on tiny invertebrates. Several species are restricted to isolated mountain ranges in Africa.

children.name: Panaspis africana
children.description: A small, slender skink of West African forests, from Sierra Leone to Cameroon. Fused transparent lower eyelid gives the eye a snake-like appearance. Forages among leaf litter on the forest floor for termites and small arthropods.

children.name: Panaspis nimbaensis
children.description: A small snake-eyed skink endemic to the Mount Nimba region of Guinea, Côte d'Ivoire, and Liberia, a UNESCO World Heritage site. Restricted to high-altitude forest above 1,000 m and threatened by iron ore mining in the region.

children.name: Panaspis cabindae
children.description: A small, secretive snake-eyed skink of Central Africa, from Cameroon through Gabon to Angola. Named after the Cabinda exclave of Angola. Found in lowland rainforest leaf litter where it browses for tiny invertebrates.

children.name: Lamprolepis
children.description: A genus of brilliantly coloured arboreal skinks found across Southeast Asia and the Pacific islands. Lamprolepis are among the most strikingly coloured skinks, with vivid green, blue, or turquoise dorsal coloration that provides camouflage in the forest canopy. They are unusual among skinks in being specialised for living on vertical tree trunks.

children.name: Lamprolepis smaragdina
children.description: A brilliantly emerald-green arboreal skink found from Palau and the Philippines through New Guinea to the Solomon Islands. Active on tree trunks in lowland and montane forests, where its green colouration provides superb camouflage among leaves and moss. One of the few truly arboreal skinks with adhesive toe pads.

children.name: Lamprolepis niepeltzii
children.description: A bright green arboreal skink from the Philippines, named after the herpetologist Richard Niepeltz. Found on forest tree trunks in the Visayas and Mindanao regions. Distinguished from L. smaragdina by subtle differences in head scale morphology.

children.name: Isopachys
children.description: A genus of small, limbless or nearly limbless burrowing skinks found only in Southeast Asia. They are among the most extreme examples of limb reduction in skinks, with a serpentine body adapted for moving through soil and leaf litter. The eyes are reduced and covered by a transparent scale.

children.name: Isopachys gyldenstolpei
children.description: A limbless burrowing skink endemic to Thailand, named after Swedish explorer and diplomat Nils Gyldenstolpe. Virtually no external limb vestiges remain; the body is long, slender, and cylindrical. Found in deep leaf litter and loose soil of dry dipterocarp forests.

children.name: Isopachys anguicinus
children.description: A highly specialised burrowing skink of Thailand and Myanmar, with a snake-like appearance and only remnant hind limb flaps. Inhabits moist lowland forests where it burrows through loose, humus-rich soil.

children.name: Isopachys borneensis
children.description: A rarely seen limbless skink from the lowland rainforests of Borneo. The entire body is adapted for a fossorial existence, with a smooth, polished surface for reduced friction. Known only from a few specimens.

children.name: Brachymeles
children.description: A Philippine-endemic genus of burrowing skinks showing one of the most complete evolutionary series in limb reduction — from fully limbed species through various intermediate states to completely limbless forms. They are a textbook example of convergent evolution with the Australian Lerista radiation. Endemic to the Philippines.

children.name: Brachymeles bonitae
children.description: A small, short-legged burrowing skink from Luzon in the Philippines, named for the descriptive 'pretty' (bonita) in Spanish. Unlike many Brachymeles, it retains functional but short pentadactyl limbs. Inhabits loose forest soil and leaf litter.

children.name: Brachymeles gracilis
children.description: A moderately limb-reduced Brachymeles from Luzon, with slender body and reduced but functional legs. Inhabits lowland and montane forest soils. Endemic to the Philippines like all Brachymeles.

children.name: Brachymeles tridactylus
children.description: A limb-reduced Brachymeles with only three toes, endemic to Luzon. Exemplifies the series of limb reduction within the genus. Lives in deep leaf litter and topsoil in montane forest.

children.name: Riopa
children.description: A genus of supple, secretive skinks from South and Southeast Asia with fused eyelids giving a snake-like gaze. They have reduced limbs and elongate bodies adapted for moving through leaf litter and loose soil. Formerly included within Lygosoma but resurrected following molecular studies.

children.name: Riopa punctata
children.description: A small supple skink of India, Sri Lanka, and Bangladesh, with a series of dark dots on the flanks. Reduced limbs and a flexible body allow it to move through leaf litter. Common in gardens and cultivated areas where it hunts small insects.

children.name: Riopa blythi
children.description: A slender, snake-like skink of Myanmar and the Malay Peninsula, named after Edward Blyth. The limbs are highly reduced and almost non-functional. Inhabits lowland rainforests where it burrows through leaf litter and topsoil.

children.name: Riopa bowringii
children.description: A supple skink of southern China, Myanmar, Thailand, and Vietnam, named after John Charles Bowring, British consul and naturalist. Inhabits forest floors and bamboo groves. Nocturnal in habits, unusual for skinks.
